10 AI Risks in Higher Education and Mitigation Strategies
Skip to content
Infographics
02/9/2024
10 AI Risks in Higher Education and Strategies for Mitigation
Why Read This Insight
When integrating AI into educational models and administrative processes, it is critical to balance its benefits with the potential downsides. Explore this infographic of risks that can hinder the sustainable and ethical adoption of AI in higher education and learn how to mitigate them.

Area in Focus:
Artificial Intelligence

sources.title: The biggest risk of AI in higher education isn’t cheating |
sources.snippet:
The biggest risk of AI in higher education isn’t cheating | The Independent

Public debate about
artificial intelligence
in
higher education
has largely orbited a familiar worry: cheating. Will
students
use chatbots to write essays? Can instructors tell? Should universities ban the tech? Embrace it?
These concerns are understandable. But focusing so much on cheating misses the larger transformation already underway, one that extends far beyond student misconduct and even the classroom.
Universities are adopting AI across many areas of institutional life. Some uses are largely invisible, like systems that help allocate resources, flag “at-risk” students, optimize course scheduling or automate routine administrative decisions.
Other uses are more noticeable. Students use AI tools to summarize and study, instructors use them to build assignments and syllabuses and researchers use them to write code, scan literature and compress hours of tedious work into minutes.
People may use AI to cheat or skip out on work assignments. But the many uses of AI in higher
education
, and the changes they portend, beg a much deeper question: As machines become more capable of doing the labor of research and learning, what happens to higher education? What purpose does the university serve?
Cognitive psychology has shown that students grow intellectually through doing the work of drafting, revising, failing, trying again, grappling with confusion and revising weak arguments
(
Getty Images
)
Over the past eight years, we’ve been studying the moral implications of pervasive engagement with AI as part of a joint research project between the Applied Ethics Center at UMass Boston and the Institute for Ethics and Emerging Technologies.
In a recent white paper, we argue that as AI systems become more autonomous, the ethical stakes of AI use in higher ed rise, as do its potential consequences.
As these technologies become better at producing knowledge work – designing classes, writing papers, suggesting experiments and summarizing difficult texts – they don’t just make universities more productive.
They risk hollowing out the ecosystem of learning and mentorship upon which these institutions are built, and on which they depend.
Nonautonomous AI
Consider three kinds of AI systems and their respective impacts on university life:
AI-powered software is already being used throughout higher education in admissions review, purchasing, academic advising and institutional risk assessment. These are considered “nonautonomous” systems because they automate tasks

The greatest risk of AI in higher education isn't cheating—it's the erosion of learning itself

The greatest risk of AI in higher education isn't cheating—it's the erosion of learning itself
by Nir Eisikovits, Jacob Burley,
The Conversation
edited by
Gaby Clark
, 																								reviewed by
Andrew Zinin

editorial process
and
policies
.

														the following attributes while ensuring the content's credibility:

Credit: Pixabay/CC0 Public Domain
Public debate about artificial intelligence in higher education has largely orbited a familiar worry:
cheating
. Will students use chatbots to write essays? Can instructors tell? Should universities ban the tech? Embrace it?
These concerns are understandable. But focusing so much on cheating misses the larger transformation already underway, one that extends far beyond student misconduct and even the classroom.
Universities are adopting AI
across many areas of institutional life
. Some uses are largely invisible, like systems that help allocate resources,
flag "at-risk" students
, optimize course scheduling, or automate routine administrative decisions. Other uses are more noticeable. Students use AI tools to summarize and study, instructors use them to build assignments and syllabuses, and researchers use them to write code, scan literature, and compress hours of tedious work into minutes.
People may use AI to cheat or skip out on work assignments. But the many uses of AI in higher education, and the changes they portend, beg a much deeper question: As machines become more capable of doing the labor of research and learning, what happens to higher education? What purpose does the university serve?
Over the past eight years, we've been studying the moral implications of pervasive engagement with AI as part of a joint research project between the
Applied Ethics Center at UMass Boston
and the
Institute for Ethics and Emerging Technologies
. In a
recent white paper
, we argue that as AI systems become more autonomous, the ethical stakes of AI use in higher ed rise, as do its potential consequences.
As these technologies become better at producing knowledge work—designing classes, writing papers, suggesting experiments, and summarizing difficult texts—they don't just make universities more productive. They risk hollowing out the ecosystem of learning and mentorship upon which these institutions are built, and on which they depend.
Nonautonomous AI
Consider three kinds of AI systems and their respective impacts on university life:
AI-powered software is already being used throughout higher education in
admissions review
, purchasing,
academic advising
, and
institutional risk assessment
. These are considered "nonautonomous" systems because they automate tasks,

sources.snippet:
The greatest risk of AI in higher education isn’t cheating – it’s the erosion of learning itself - Cobb Courier
Posted By:
Nor
February 27, 2026
by
Nir Eisikovits
,
UMass Boston
and
Jacob Burley
,
UMass Boston
, [This article first appeared in The Conversation, republished with permission]
Public debate about artificial intelligence in higher education has largely orbited a familiar worry:
cheating
. Will students use chatbots to write essays? Can instructors tell? Should universities ban the tech? Embrace it?
These concerns are understandable. But focusing so much on cheating misses the larger transformation already underway, one that extends far beyond student misconduct and even the classroom.
Universities are adopting AI
across many areas of institutional life
. Some uses are largely invisible, like systems that help allocate resources,
flag “at-risk” students
, optimize course scheduling or automate routine administrative decisions. Other uses are more noticeable. Students use AI tools to summarize and study, instructors use them to build assignments and syllabuses and researchers use them to write code, scan literature and compress hours of tedious work into minutes.
People may use AI to cheat or skip out on work assignments. But the many uses of AI in higher education, and the changes they portend, beg a much deeper question: As machines become more capable of doing the labor of research and learning, what happens to higher education? What purpose does the university serve?
Over the past eight years, we’ve been studying the moral implications of pervasive engagement with AI as part of a joint research project between the
Applied Ethics Center at UMass Boston
and the
Institute for Ethics and Emerging Technologies
. In a
recent white paper
, we argue that as AI systems become more autonomous, the ethical stakes of AI use in higher ed rise, as do its potential consequences.
As these technologies become better at producing knowledge work – designing classes, writing papers, suggesting experiments and summarizing difficult texts – they don’t just make universities more productive. They risk hollowing out the ecosystem of learning and mentorship upon which these institutions are built, and on which they depend.
Nonautonomous AI
Consider three kinds of AI systems and their respective impacts on university life:
AI-powered software is already being used throughout higher education in
admissions review
, purchasing,
academic advising
and institutional risk assessment. These are considered “nonautonomous” systems because they automate tasks, but a person is “in the loop” and using these systems as tools.
These technologies can
pose a risk to students’ privacy and data security
. They also can be biased. And they often lack sufficient transparency to determine the sources of these problems. Who has access to student data? How are “risk scores” generated? How do we prevent systems from reproducing inequities or treating certain students as problems to

How Artificial Intelligence Will Change the World
Nexford Team
Published on January 25, 2026
|
10 minute read
You would have been living under a rock if you did not know how artificial intelligence is set to affect jobs in 2026-2030. AI like ChatGPT seems to be stealing all of the headlines at the moment, Google unveiled new AI software to build presentations, analyze and enter data, and write content, and there are so many more AI tools like Gamma and Numerous AI.  Those that are resisting, rather than riding the crest of the wave will not be making hey whilst the sun shines when it comes to landing in-demand jobs in the next 6 years and enjoying job growth. AI will be taking some jobs, but it will be creating new ones!   Here are the most likely jobs that artificial intelligence will affect from 2026-2030:
BACK TO BLOG PAGE
How Artificial Intelligence Will Change the World
Nexford Team
Published on January 25, 2026
10 minute read
Share this article:
Copy to clipboard
How artificial intelligence will change the world
Will AI help the world or hurt it?
Like any controversial subject, there will always be people who are for it, and those that are against it. Artificial Intelligence is no different. In fact, as new ai tools are introduced, and the news around them grows, so the division between the two camps will grow with it.
Many market research analysts say that AI has the potential to bring about numerous positive changes in society, including enhanced productivity, improved healthcare, and increased access to education. But we need to adapt right now.
Others will say, mostly those working in human work types of jobs that are manually repetitive, that ai and robotics is a disruptive force and when it comes to the future of jobs it merely serves to steal jobs.
But robots and ai technologies can and will create a great many new vocations and help solve complex problems and make our daily lives easier and more convenient.
The jury is not yet out on this, but the leaning is more toward ai being a positive force rather than a negative one.
How will AI affect jobs and the economy?
McKinsey global institute says that at the global average level of adoption and absorption and advances in ai implied by their simulation, AI has the profound impact to deliver additional global economic activity of around $13 tr

sources.title: Postgraduate students’ perceptionsofartificialintelligence...
sources.snippet:
Postgraduate students’ perceptions of artificial intelligence integration in research: A cross-sectional study | PLOS One
Browse Subject Areas
?
Click through the PLOS taxonomy to find articles in your field.
For more information about PLOS Subject Areas, click
here
.
Article
Authors
Metrics
Comments
Media Coverage
Reader Comments
Figures
Figures
Abstract
Background
Generative artificial intelligence (AI) tools such as ChatGPT are increasingly used in academic research, yet evidence on postgraduate students’ perceptions remains limited in non-Western and health-professional contexts. Understanding how students perceive AI’s benefits, risks, and ethical implications is essential for informing institutional research policies.
Methods
This cross-sectional case study surveyed 267 master’s students enrolled in nursing and health profession programs at Northern Border University in Arar, Saudi Arabia. Data were collected between October 1 and November 15, 2025, using a validated 54-item questionnaire that assessed perceived benefits, perceived risks, privacy concerns, mistrust in AI, performance anxiety, social bias, regulatory matters, liability issues, and intention to adopt AI tools. Multiple linear regression with heteroscedasticity-robust (HC3) standard errors was used to identify predictors of AI adoption intention.
Results
Most participants (85.0%) reported prior use of AI tools, predominantly ChatGPT. Perceived benefits were the strongest predictor of intention to adopt AI for research purposes (β = 0.588, p < 0.001). Privacy concerns were positively associated with adoption intention (β = 0.230, p < 0.001), suggesting informed and critical engagement rather than resistance. Female students reported higher adoption intention than males (β = 0.137, p = 0.002), while prior publication experience was negatively associated with intention (β = −0.089, p = 0.036). Demographic variables such as age, specialty, and marital status were not significant predictors. The adoption-intention model demonstrated moderate explanatory power (adjusted R
2
= 0.560).
Conclusions
Among nursing and health profession master’s students at a regional Saudi university, findings indicate pragmatic optimism toward AI integration in academic research, driven primarily by perceived benefits alongside heightened ethical and privacy awareness. Privacy concerns appear to reflect critical literacy rather than barriers to adoption.
Citation:
Alenezi IN, Mersal FA, Elbilgahy AA (2026) Postgraduate students’ perceptions of artificial intelligence integration in research: A cross-sectional study. PLoS One 21(3):
           e0345726.
        
        https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0345726
Editor:
Stephen R. Milford, University of Basel Institute for Biomedical Ethics: Universitat Basel Institut fur Bio- und Medizinethik, SWITZERLAND

sources.title: ETHICAL Principles AI Framework for Higher Education
sources.snippet:
ETHICAL Principles AI Framework for Higher Education | CSU AI Commons
Skip to main content
ETHICAL Principles AI Framework for Higher Education
by Wynants, S., Childers, G., De La Torre Roman, Y. , Budar-Turner, D., & Vasquez, P. (2025) at California State University, Fullerton. Licensed under
CC BY-NC 4.0
This link will take you to an external website in a new tab.
Introduction
The ETHICAL Principles AI Framework for Higher Education is designed to provide a flexible and adaptable foundation for the responsible use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) across diverse academic contexts. This framework recognizes the rapidly evolving nature of AI technologies and the varying needs of different departments, colleges, and institutions.
Our goal is to offer a set of guiding principles that can be readily interpreted and applied to individual circumstances, ensuring relevance and utility as AI continues to advance. The framework intentionally avoids rigid definitions, allowing users to contextualize and adapt these principles to their unique environments, academic settings, and as technologies change.
By providing this framework under a Creative Commons license, we encourage its widespread use, modification, and adaptation. We invite departments, colleges, and other institutions to tailor these principles to their specific needs, fostering a culture of responsible AI use that aligns with their values and objectives.
It is important to note that this framework is not prescriptive. Instead, it serves as a starting point for developing more detailed policies, guidelines, and educational programs. The specific implementation of these principles, including how to address potential AI limitations and biases, should be determined by individual units or institutions based on their unique contexts and evolving understanding of AI technologies.
As laws and regulations surrounding AI-generated content and its use in academia continue to develop, this framework provides a stable ethical foundation that can accommodate future legal and technological changes. We encourage users of this framework to stay informed about current developments and to interpret these principles in light of the most up-to-date information available.
By adopting and adapting this ETHICAL Principles AI Framework, institutions can foster a thoughtful, responsible, inclusive, and innovative approach to AI integration in higher education, promoting ethical considerations while embracing the potential of these transformative technologies.
What follows is an accessible infographic of the framework, and then a more detailed description of each of the ethical principles of the framework, ending with recommended practical application strategies for implementing the framework across an institution.
Image
This image is described in full below.
ETHICAL Principles AI Framework for Higher Education
Exploration and Evaluation

sources.title: Ethical Challenges Associated with the Use of Artificial Intelligence ...
sources.snippet:
Ethical Challenges Associated with the Use of Artificial Intelligence in University Education | Journal of Academic Ethics | Springer Nature Link
Skip to main content
Advertisement
Ethical Challenges Associated with the Use of Artificial Intelligence in University Education
Open access
Published:
01 July 2025
Volume 23
, pages 2443–2467, (
2025
)
Cite this article
You have full access to this
open access
article
Download PDF
Save article
View saved research
Journal of Academic Ethics
Aims and scope
Submit manuscript
Ethical Challenges Associated with the Use of Artificial Intelligence in University Education
Download PDF
Abstract
The integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) within higher education has given rise to substantial ethical concerns and challenges, including concerns regarding data privacy, equity in educational resources, and algorithmic biases. These factors have the potential to compromise the integrity of academic processes. In light of this, the study aims to analyze the perceptions of students and faculty members about the ethical challenges associated with the use of AI in universities. The methodology employed was non-experimental quantitative, with the questionnaires being designed according to Luciano Floridi’s algorithmic theory. The study’s participants 890 university students and 162 faculty members from 21 higher education institutions in Peru. Results indicate that 51.2% of faculty and 47.5% of students expressed concern about data privacy and security. Moreover, a significant proportion of respondents, 61.1% of faculty and 53.5% of students, believed that AI systems lack transparency. These findings highlight the urgent need for regulatory frameworks that promote ethical AI use, ensure equitable access, and safeguard academic autonomy. It is essential for universities to implement concrete measures, including ethical oversight mechanisms, audit protocols, and digital literacy training programs, to maximize the benefits and mitigate the risks of AI in education.

themes.name: Risk of Eroding Learning and Mentorship
themes.summary: The integration of AI in higher education poses a significant risk to the erosion of learning and mentorship, potentially hollowing out the ecosystem of learning and mentorship in universities. This risk is more significant than the commonly discussed issue of cheating, and it highlights the need for careful consideration of the implications of AI on the core functions of universities.

themes.name: Need for Ethical Considerations and Regulatory Frameworks
themes.summary: The adoption of AI in higher education raises significant ethical concerns, including data privacy and security, lack of transparency, and potential biases. There is a need for regulatory frameworks and concrete measures to mitigate these risks and ensure responsible AI use in higher education.

themes.name: Transformative Potential of AI in Education
themes.summary: AI has the potential to bring about positive changes in higher education, including improved access to education and increased productivity. However, this potential must be carefully balanced against the potential risks and challenges associated with AI adoption.

themes.name: Importance of Diversity, Inclusion, and International Collaboration
themes.summary: The development and adoption of AI in higher education must prioritize diversity, inclusion, and international collaboration to ensure that AI systems are fair, transparent, and effective. This includes addressing potential biases and ensuring that AI education is accessible to students from diverse backgrounds.

themes.name: Need for Critical Assessment and Evaluation of AI Tools
themes.summary: Higher education institutions must critically assess and evaluate AI tools and outputs to ensure they are reliable, accurate, and unbiased. This includes addressing potential limitations and biases in AI systems and ensuring that AI-driven decision-making processes are transparent and fair.

themes.name: Institutional Responsibility and Proactive Measures
themes.summary: Higher education institutions have a responsibility to implement concrete measures to mitigate AI risks, including ethical oversight mechanisms, audit protocols, and digital literacy training programs. This requires proactive measures to ensure that AI is used in ways that support, rather than undermine, academic values and integrity.

contradictions.description: The greatest risk of AI in higher education
contradictions.resolution: The claims suggest that the greatest risk of AI in higher education is the erosion of learning itself, not cheating. This could be due to AI's potential to automate tasks, replace human labor and mentorship, and hollow out the ecosystem of learning and mentorship. To reconcile this, higher education institutions could focus on developing strategies to mitigate these risks, such as implementing AI in a way that complements human instruction and mentorship, rather than replacing it.

contradictions.description: The impact of AI on jobs in higher education
contradictions.resolution: The claims suggest that AI will both take and create jobs in higher education, and that resistance to AI will hinder career prospects. To reconcile this, higher education institutions could focus on providing training and upskilling programs for educators and students to adapt to the changing job market, and emphasize the development of skills that are complementary to AI, such as critical thinking, creativity, and emotional intelligence.

contradictions.description: The importance of diversity and inclusion in AI education
contradictions.resolution: The claims suggest that diversity and inclusion are essential in AI education, and that female students are more likely to adopt AI tools for research purposes. To reconcile this, higher education institutions could focus on promoting diversity and inclusion in AI education, and provide support and resources for underrepresented groups to access and utilize AI tools effectively.

contradictions.description: The need for regulatory frameworks and ethical guidelines for AI use in higher education
contradictions.resolution: The claims suggest that AI poses a threat to academic integrity, and that ethical principles and regulatory frameworks are necessary to guide AI use in education. To reconcile this, higher education institutions could focus on developing and implementing ethical guidelines and regulatory frameworks for AI use, and provide training and support for educators and students to use AI in a responsible and ethical manner.

report_outline: Risk of Eroding Learning and Mentorship
Need for Ethical Considerations and Regulatory Frameworks
Transformative Potential of AI in Education
Importance of Diversity, Inclusion, and International Collaboration
Need for Critical Assessment and Evaluation of AI Tools
final_report:
# AI Risks in Higher Education
## Introduction
The integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in higher education poses significant risks and challenges that need to be addressed. While AI has the potential to bring about positive changes in higher education, including improved access to education and increased productivity [21, 22, 25, 26, 27, 28], it also raises ethical concerns, including data privacy and security, lack of transparency, and potential biases [14, 15, 40, 41, 42, 43, 44, 45, 46]. This report will examine the risks of AI in higher education, the need for ethical considerations and regulatory frameworks, and the importance of diversity, inclusion, and international collaboration in AI education.

## Risk of Eroding Learning and Mentorship
The biggest risk of AI in higher education is not cheating, but rather the erosion of learning itself [3, 7, 11, 16]. As AI systems become more autonomous, they risk hollowing out the ecosystem of learning and mentorship in universities [5, 9, 10, 13, 19]. This is because AI systems can automate tasks and make universities more productive, but they also risk undermining the intellectual growth of students [4]. The use of AI in higher education raises significant ethical stakes and potential consequences, particularly as AI systems become more autonomous [5, 9, 18].

## Need for Ethical Considerations and Regulatory Frameworks
The adoption of AI in higher education raises significant ethical concerns, including data privacy and security, lack of transparency, and potential biases [14, 15, 40, 41, 42, 43, 44, 45, 46]. There is a need for regulatory frameworks and concrete measures to mitigate these risks and ensure responsible AI use in higher education [45, 46]. Ethical principles are necessary to guide AI use in education, and universities must implement concrete measures to mitigate AI risks, including ethical oversight mechanisms, audit protocols, and digital literacy training programs [46].

## Transformative Potential of AI in Education
AI has the potential to bring about positive changes in higher education, including improved access to education and increased productivity [21, 22, 25, 26, 27, 28]. Higher education institutions are developing specialized AI programs to prepare students for future challenges [28]. AI can also improve academic research, and postgraduate students perceive benefits of AI integration in academic research [32].

## Importance of Diversity, Inclusion, and International Collaboration
The development and adoption of AI in higher education must prioritize diversity, inclusion, and international collaboration to ensure that AI systems are fair, transparent, and effective [29, 31, 34]. Female students are more likely to adopt AI tools for research purposes, and international collaboration is essential in AI education to address global challenges [31, 34].

## Need for Critical Assessment and Evaluation of AI Tools
Higher education institutions must critically assess and evaluate AI tools and outputs to ensure they are reliable, accurate, and unbiased [32, 33, 35, 36, 37, 38, 39]. AI limitations and biases must be addressed in higher education, and institutions must stay informed about AI-related laws and regulations [37, 38].

## Institutional Responsibility and Proactive Measures
Higher education institutions have a responsibility to implement concrete measures to mitigate AI risks, including ethical oversight mechanisms, audit protocols, and digital literacy training programs [46]. Universities must also prioritize diversity, inclusion, and international collaboration in AI education and provide support and resources for underrepresented groups to access and utilize AI tools effectively.

## Discussion
The integration of AI in higher education poses significant risks and challenges that need to be addressed. While AI has the potential to bring about positive changes in higher education, it also raises ethical concerns and requires regulatory frameworks and concrete measures to mitigate these risks. Higher education institutions must prioritize diversity, inclusion, and international collaboration in AI education and provide support and resources for underrepresented groups to access and utilize AI tools effectively.

## Limitations
This report has several limitations. First, the sources used in this report are limited to online articles and academic papers, and may not reflect the full range of perspectives on AI risks in higher education. Second, the report focuses primarily on the risks and challenges associated with AI adoption in higher education, and may not fully capture the potential benefits of AI in education. Finally, the report does not provide a comprehensive analysis of the regulatory frameworks and concrete measures that can be taken to mitigate AI risks in higher education.

## Conclusion
The integration of AI in higher education poses significant risks and challenges that need to be addressed. While AI has the potential to bring about positive changes in higher education, it also raises ethical concerns and requires regulatory frameworks and concrete measures to mitigate these risks. Higher education institutions must prioritize diversity, inclusion, and international collaboration in AI education and provide support and resources for underrepresented groups to access and utilize AI tools effectively.

2. The biggest risk of AI in higher education isn’t cheating | — https://www.independent.co.uk/tech/ai-higher-education-cheating-students-professors-b2923760.html
3. The greatest risk of AI in higher education isn't — https://phys.org/news/2026-02-greatest-ai-higher-isnt-erosion.html
4. The greatest risk of AI in higher education isn’t cheating — https://cobbcountycourier.com/2026/02/the-greatest-risk-of-ai-in-higher-education-isnt-cheating-its-the-erosion-of-learning-itself/
5. The greatest risk of AI in higher education isn’t cheating — https://circularsymphony.com/the-greatest-risk-of-ai-in-higher-education-isnt-cheating-its-the-erosion-of-learning-itself/
6. Environmental impact of artificial intelligence - Wikipedia — https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Environmental_impact_of_artificial_intelligence

evaluation.reasoning: The report provides a comprehensive overview of the risks and challenges associated with AI in higher education, covering key aspects such as the erosion of learning, ethical concerns, and the need for regulatory frameworks. The report is largely faithful to the evidence provided, with most claims supported by references to the available sources. However, there are some instances of slight overstatement or extrapolation, resulting in a minor hallucination rate. The report is well-structured, clear, and relevant, making it useful for decision-making in the context of AI in higher education.
